function.name: clarify
function.description:
Ask the user a question when you need clarification, feedback, or a decision before proceeding. Supports two modes:

1. **Multiple choice** — provide up to 4 choices. The user picks one or types their own answer via a 5th 'Other' option.
2. **Open-ended** — omit choices entirely. The user types a free-form response.

Use this tool when:
- The task is ambiguous and you need the user to choose an approach
- You want post-task feedback ('How did that work out?')
- You want to offer to save a skill or update memory
- A decision has meaningful trade-offs the user should weigh in on

Do NOT use this tool for simple yes/no confirmation of dangerous commands (the terminal tool handles that). Prefer making a reasonable default choice yourself when the decision is low-stakes.

question.description: The question to present to the user.

choices.description: Up to 4 answer choices. Omit this parameter entirely to ask an open-ended question. When provided, the UI automatically appends an 'Other (type your answer)' option.

function.name: cronjob
function.description:
Manage scheduled cron jobs with a single compressed tool.

Use action='create' to schedule a new job from a prompt or one or more skills.
Use action='list' to inspect jobs.
Use action='update', 'pause', 'resume', 'remove', or 'run' to manage an existing job.

Jobs run in a fresh session with no current-chat context, so prompts must be self-contained.
If skill or skills are provided on create, the future cron run loads those skills in order, then follows the prompt as the task instruction.
On update, passing skills=[] clears attached skills.

If script is provided on create, the referenced Python script runs before each agent turn.
Its stdout is injected into the prompt as context. Use this for data collection and change
detection — the script handles gathering data, the agent analyzes and reports.
On update, pass script="" to clear an attached script.

NOTE: The agent's final response is auto-delivered to the target. Put the primary
user-facing content in the final response. Cron jobs run autonomously with no user
present — they cannot ask questions or request clarification.

Important safety rule: cron-run sessions should not recursively schedule more cron jobs.

action.description: One of: create, list, update, pause, resume, remove, run

prompt.description: For create: the full self-contained prompt. If skill or skills are also provided, this becomes the task instruction paired with those skills.

repeat.description: Optional repeat count. Omit for defaults (once for one-shot, forever for recurring).

deliver.description: Delivery target: origin, local, telegram, discord, slack, whatsapp, signal, matrix, mattermost, homeassistant, dingtalk, feishu, wecom, email, sms, or platform:chat_id or platform:chat_id:thread_id for Telegram topics. Examples: 'origin', 'local', 'telegram', 'telegram:-[REDACTED_PHONE]:17585', 'discord:#engineering'

model.description: Optional per-job model override used when the cron job runs

provider.description: Optional per-job provider override used when resolving runtime credentials

base_url.description: Optional per-job base URL override paired with provider/model routing

skill.description: Optional single skill name to load before executing the cron prompt

skills.description: Optional ordered list of skills to load before executing the cron prompt. On update, pass an empty array to clear attached skills.

script.description: Optional path to a Python script that runs before each cron job execution. Its stdout is injected into the prompt as context. Use for data collection and change detection. Relative paths resolve under ~/.hermes/scripts/. On update, pass empty string to clear.

function.name: delegate_task
function.description:
Spawn one or more subagents to work on tasks in isolated contexts. Each subagent gets its own conversation, terminal session, and toolset. Only the final summary is returned -- intermediate tool results never enter your context window.

TWO MODES (one of 'goal' or 'tasks' is required):
1. Single task: provide 'goal' (+ optional context, toolsets)
2. Batch (parallel): provide 'tasks' array with up to 3 items. All run concurrently and results are returned together.

WHEN TO USE delegate_task:
- Reasoning-heavy subtasks (debugging, code review, research synthesis)
- Tasks that would flood your context with intermediate data
- Parallel independent workstreams (research A and B simultaneously)

WHEN NOT TO USE (use these instead):
- Mechanical multi-step work with no reasoning needed -> use execute_code
- Single tool call -> just call the tool directly
- Tasks needing user interaction -> subagents cannot use clarify

IMPORTANT:
- Subagents have NO memory of your conversation. Pass all relevant info (file paths, error messages, constraints) via the 'context' field.
- Subagents CANNOT call: delegate_task, clarify, memory, send_message, execute_code.
- Each subagent gets its own terminal session (separate working directory and state).
- Results are always returned as an array, one entry per task.

goal.description: What the subagent should accomplish. Be specific and self-contained -- the subagent knows nothing about your conversation history.

context.description: Background information the subagent needs: file paths, error messages, project structure, constraints. The more specific you are, the better the subagent performs.

toolsets.description: Toolsets to enable for this subagent. Default: inherits your enabled toolsets. Common patterns: ['terminal', 'file'] for code work, ['web'] for research, ['terminal', 'file', 'web'] for full-stack tasks.
Toolsets for this specific task. Use 'web' for network access, 'terminal' for shell.

acp_command.description: Per-task ACP command override (e.g. 'claude'). Overrides the top-level acp_command for this task only.

tasks.description: Batch mode: up to 3 tasks to run in parallel. Each gets its own subagent with isolated context and terminal session. When provided, top-level goal/context/toolsets are ignored.

max_iterations.description: Max tool-calling turns per subagent (default: 50). Only set lower for simple tasks.

acp_command.description: Override ACP command for child agents (e.g. 'claude', 'copilot'). When set, children use ACP subprocess transport instead of inheriting the parent's transport. Enables spawning Claude Code (claude --acp --stdio) or other ACP-capable agents from any parent, including Discord/Telegram/CLI.

acp_args.description: Arguments for the ACP command (default: ['--acp', '--stdio']). Only used when acp_command is set. Example: ['--acp', '--stdio', '--model', 'claude-opus-4-6']

function.name: execute_code
function.description:
Run a Python script that can call Hermes tools programmatically. Use this when you need 3+ tool calls with processing logic between them, need to filter/reduce large tool outputs before they enter your context, need conditional branching (if X then Y else Z), or need to loop (fetch N pages, process N files, retry on failure).

Use normal tool calls instead when: single tool call with no processing, you need to see the full result and apply complex reasoning, or the task requires interactive user input.

Available via `from hermes_tools import ...`:

  web_search(query: str, limit: int = 5) -> dict
    Returns {"data": {"web": [{"url", "title", "description"}, ...]}}
  web_extract(urls: list[str]) -> dict
    Returns {"results": [{"url", "title", "content", "error"}, ...]} where content is markdown
  read_file(path: str, offset: int = 1, limit: int = 500) -> dict
    Lines are 1-indexed. Returns {"content": "...", "total_lines": N}
  write_file(path: str, content: str) -> dict
    Always overwrites the entire file.
  search_files(pattern: str, target="content", path=".", file_glob=None, limit=50) -> dict
    target: "content" (search inside files) or "files" (find files by name). Returns {"matches": [...]}
  patch(path: str, old_string: str, new_string: str, replace_all: bool = False) -> dict
    Replaces old_string with new_string in the file.
  terminal(command: str, timeout=None, workdir=None) -> dict
    Foreground only (no background/pty). Returns {"output": "...", "exit_code": N}

Limits: 5-minute timeout, 50KB stdout cap, max 50 tool calls per script. terminal() is foreground-only (no background or pty). If the session uses a cloud sandbox backend, treat it as resumable task state rather than a durable always-on machine.

Print your final result to stdout. Use Python stdlib (json, re, math, csv, datetime, collections, etc.) for processing between tool calls.

Also available (no import needed — built into hermes_tools):
  json_parse(text: str) — json.loads with strict=False; use for terminal() output with control chars
  shell_quote(s: str) — shlex.quote(); use when interpolating dynamic strings into shell commands
  retry(fn, max_attempts=3, delay=2) — retry with exponential backoff for transient failures

code.description: Python code to execute. Import tools with `from hermes_tools import web_search, terminal, ...` and print your final result to stdout.

function.name: [REDACTED_TOKEN]
function.description:
A detailed tool for dynamic and reflective problem-solving through thoughts.
This tool helps analyze problems through a flexible thinking process that can adapt and evolve.
Each thought can build on, question, or revise previous insights as understanding deepens.

When to use this tool:
- Breaking down complex problems into steps
- Planning and design with room for revision
- Analysis that might need course correction
- Problems where the full scope might not be clear initially
- Problems that require a multi-step solution
- Tasks that need to maintain context over multiple steps
- Situations where irrelevant information needs to be filtered out

Key features:
- You can adjust total_thoughts up or down as you progress
- You can question or revise previous thoughts
- You can add more thoughts even after reaching what seemed like the end
- You can express uncertainty and explore alternative approaches
- Not every thought needs to build linearly - you can branch or backtrack
- Generates a solution hypothesis
- Verifies the hypothesis based on the Chain of Thought steps
- Repeats the process until satisfied
- Provides a correct answer

Parameters explained:
- thought: Your current thinking step, which can include:
  * Regular analytical steps
  * Revisions of previous thoughts
  * Questions about previous decisions
  * Realizations about needing more analysis
  * Changes in approach
  * Hypothesis generation
  * Hypothesis verification
- nextThoughtNeeded: True if you need more thinking, even if at what seemed like the end
- thoughtNumber: Current number in sequence (can go beyond initial total if needed)
- totalThoughts: Current estimate of thoughts needed (can be adjusted up/down)
- isRevision: A boolean indicating if this thought revises previous thinking
- revisesThought: If is_revision is true, which thought number is being reconsidered
- branchFromThought: If branching, which thought number is the branching point
- branchId: Identifier for the current branch (if any)
- needsMoreThoughts: If reaching end but realizing more thoughts needed

You should:
1. Start with an initial estimate of needed thoughts, but be ready to adjust
2. Feel free to question or revise previous thoughts
3. Don't hesitate to add more thoughts if needed, even at the "end"
4. Express uncertainty when present
5. Mark thoughts that revise previous thinking or branch into new paths
6. Ignore information that is irrelevant to the current step
7. Generate a solution hypothesis when appropriate
8. Verify the hypothesis based on the Chain of Thought steps
9. Repeat the process until satisfied with the solution
10. Provide a single, ideally correct answer as the final output
11. Only set nextThoughtNeeded to false when truly done and a satisfactory answer is reached

nextThoughtNeeded.description: Whether another thought step is needed

totalThoughts.description: Estimated total thoughts needed (numeric value, e.g., 5, 10)

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: mixture_of_agents
function.description: Route a hard problem through multiple frontier LLMs collaboratively. Makes 5 API calls (4 reference models + 1 aggregator) with maximum reasoning effort — use sparingly for genuinely difficult problems. Best for: complex math, advanced algorithms, multi-step analytical reasoning, problems benefiting from diverse perspectives.

user_prompt.description: The complex query or problem to solve using multiple AI models. Should be a challenging problem that benefits from diverse perspectives and collaborative reasoning.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: send_message
function.description:
Send a message to a connected messaging platform, or list available targets.

IMPORTANT: When the user asks to send to a specific channel or person (not just a bare platform name), call send_message(action='list') FIRST to see available targets, then send to the correct one.
If the user just says a platform name like 'send to telegram', send directly to the home channel without listing first.

action.description: Action to perform. 'send' (default) sends a message. 'list' returns all available channels/contacts across connected platforms.

target.description: Delivery target. Format: 'platform' (uses home channel), 'platform:#channel-name', 'platform:chat_id', or Telegram topic 'telegram:chat_id:thread_id'. Examples: 'telegram', 'telegram:-[REDACTED_PHONE]:17585', 'discord:#bot-home', 'slack:#engineering', 'signal:[REDACTED_PHONE]'

function.name: session_search
function.description:
Search your long-term memory of past conversations, or browse recent sessions. This is your recall -- every past session is searchable, and this tool summarizes what happened.

TWO MODES:
1. Recent sessions (no query): Call with no arguments to see what was worked on recently. Returns titles, previews, and timestamps. Zero LLM cost, instant. Start here when the user asks what were we working on or what did we do recently.
2. Keyword search (with query): Search for specific topics across all past sessions. Returns LLM-generated summaries of matching sessions.

USE THIS PROACTIVELY when:
- The user says 'we did this before', 'remember when', 'last time', 'as I mentioned'
- The user asks about a topic you worked on before but don't have in current context
- The user references a project, person, or concept that seems familiar but isn't in memory
- You want to check if you've solved a similar problem before
- The user asks 'what did we do about X?' or 'how did we fix Y?'

Don't hesitate to search when it is actually cross-session -- it's fast and cheap. Better to search and confirm than to guess or ask the user to repeat themselves.

Search syntax: keywords joined with OR for broad recall (elevenlabs OR baseten OR funding), phrases for exact match ("docker networking"), boolean (python NOT java), prefix (deploy*). IMPORTANT: Use OR between keywords for best results — FTS5 defaults to AND which misses sessions that only mention some terms. If a broad OR query returns nothing, try individual keyword searches in parallel. Returns summaries of the top matching sessions.

query.description: Search query — keywords, phrases, or boolean expressions to find in past sessions. Omit this parameter entirely to browse recent sessions instead (returns titles, previews, timestamps with no LLM cost).

role_filter.description: Optional: only search messages from specific roles (comma-separated). E.g. 'user,assistant' to skip tool outputs.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills)

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for everything that finishes.
Background: ONLY for long-running servers, watchers, or processes that never exit. Set background=true to get a session_id, then use process(action="wait") to block until done — it returns instantly on completion, same as foreground. Use process(action="poll") only when you need a progress check without blocking.
Do NOT use background for scripts, builds, or installs — foreground with a generous timeout is always better (fewer tool calls, instant results).
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: ONLY for servers/watchers that never exit. For scripts, builds, installs — use foreground with timeout instead (it returns instantly when done).

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

function.name: text_to_speech
function.description: Convert text to speech audio. Returns a MEDIA: path that the platform delivers as a voice message. On Telegram it plays as a voice bubble, on Discord/WhatsApp as an audio attachment. In CLI mode, saves to ~/voice-memos/. Voice and provider are user-configured, not model-selected.

text.description: The text to convert to speech. Keep under 4000 characters.

output_path.description: Optional custom file path to save the audio. Defaults to ~/.hermes/audio_cache/<timestamp>.mp3

function.name: todo
function.description:
Manage your task list for the current session. Use for complex tasks with 3+ steps or when the user provides multiple tasks. Call with no parameters to read the current list.

Writing:
- Provide 'todos' array to create/update items
- merge=false (default): replace the entire list with a fresh plan
- merge=true: update existing items by id, add any new ones

Each item: {id: string, content: string, status: pending|in_progress|completed|cancelled}
List order is priority. Only ONE item in_progress at a time.
Mark items completed immediately when done. If something fails, cancel it and add a revised item.

Always returns the full current list.

todos.description: Task items to write. Omit to read current list.

merge.description: true: update existing items by id, add new ones. false (default): replace the entire list.

function.name: vision_analyze
function.description: Analyze images using AI vision. Provides a comprehensive description and answers a specific question about the image content.

image_url.description: Image URL (http/https) or local file path to analyze.

question.description: Your specific question or request about the image to resolve. The AI will automatically provide a complete image description AND answer your specific question.

function.name: web_extract
function.description: Extract content from web page URLs. Returns page content in markdown format. Also works with PDF URLs (arxiv papers, documents, etc.) — pass the PDF link directly and it converts to markdown text. Pages under 5000 chars return full markdown; larger pages are LLM-summarized and capped at ~5000 chars per page. Pages over 2M chars are refused. If a URL fails or times out, use the browser tool to access it instead.

urls.description: List of URLs to extract content from (max 5 URLs per call)

function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the web for information on any topic. Returns up to 5 relevant results with titles, URLs, and descriptions.

query.description: The search query to look up on the web

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

# Solstein Autonomous Worker v2 (Karpathy Pattern)

## Philosophy (from Karpathy's autoresearch)
- TIGHT LOOPS: One small change → verify → measure → keep or discard
- NEVER commit untested code
- MEASURE everything: test count, pass rate, lint errors, LOC delta
- SHORT CHAINS: Each step must be independently verifiable
- EVALS ARE UNIT TESTS: Golden datasets protect classification boundaries

## Setup
```bash
git config --global credential.helper store
echo "https://[REDACTED_TOKEN]@github.com" > ~/.git-credentials
git config --global user.name "Hermes Agent"
git config --global user.email "[REDACTED_EMAIL]"
cd /tmp/solstein 2>/dev/null || git clone https://[REDACTED_TOKEN]@github.com/Ai-Whisperers/solstein.git /tmp/solstein
cd /tmp/solstein && git checkout develop && git pull origin develop
```

**⚠️ Git credentials setup may be blocked by security scan in cron mode.**
If the combined command is rejected, skip it — credentials are likely already stored from a prior session. Just run `cd /tmp/solstein && git pull origin develop` to verify access works.

### Dependency Installation
The Hermes venv runs Python 3.11 but system `pip` installs to Python 3.12. Always install into the venv python:
```bash
python3 -m ensurepip 2>/dev/null
python3 -m pip install -e ".[dev]"
```
This ensures factory-boy, sqlalchemy, email-validator, feedparser, fpdf2 etc. are available to the test runner.

## The Autoresearch Loop

### Step 1: MEASURE BASELINE
Before ANY changes, capture metrics:
```bash
cd /tmp/solstein
PASS_BEFORE=$(PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m pytest tests/unit/ -q --tb=no 2>&1 | tail -1)
LINT_BEFORE=$(PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m ruff check src/ 2>&1 | tail -1)
FILES_BEFORE=$(find src -name "*.py" | wc -l)
echo "BASELINE: $PASS_BEFORE | $LINT_BEFORE | $FILES_BEFORE files"
```

### Step 2: PICK WORK
Read `planning/QUEUE.md` — take the FIRST story with status `READY`.
Read the story file from `backlog/EPICS/` for context.
If a story is IN_PROGRESS from a previous session, continue it.

**⚠️ Check BLOCKED stories too!** Dependencies may have been completed since the queue was last updated. If a BLOCKED story's deps are now all DONE, update its status to READY and pick it up. Search for the dep story numbers in the queue to verify their status.

### Step 3: BRANCH
```bash
git checkout -b feature/STORY-NNN-short-desc develop
```

### Step 4: IMPLEMENT (one small change at a time)
- Read the relevant source files
- Make ONE focused change
- IMMEDIATELY lint: `PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m ruff check src/ --fix`
- IMMEDIATELY test: `PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m pytest tests/unit/ -x -q`
- If tests FAIL → revert the change, try a different approach
- If tests PASS → continue to next change
- Repeat until the story is complete

### Step 4b: LINT (MANDATORY before every commit)
```bash
PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m ruff check src/ tests/ --fix
PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m ruff format src/ tests/
```
This is NOT optional. Every commit must pass lint. Run it after EVERY change.

### Step 5: MEASURE AFTER
```bash
PASS_AFTER=$(PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m pytest tests/unit/ -q --tb=no 2>&1 | tail -1)
LINT_AFTER=$(PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m ruff check src/ 2>&1 | tail -1)
FILES_AFTER=$(find src -name "*.py" | wc -l)
echo "AFTER: $PASS_AFTER | $LINT_AFTER | $FILES_AFTER files"
```
Compare with baseline. If metrics WORSENED (more failures, more lint errors), FIX before committing.

### Step 6: COMMIT ONLY IF GREEN (lint + tests BOTH required)
```bash
PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m ruff check src/ tests/ --fix   # Fix lint FIRST
PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m ruff format src/ tests/        # Format
PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m pytest tests/unit/ -x -q       # Then test
git add -A
git commit -m "feat(STORY-NNN): <description>

Metrics: tests X passed, Y lint errors (was Z), LOC delta +/-N"
git push origin feature/STORY-NNN-short-desc
```

### Step 6.5: CREATE PR AND AUTO-MERGE
After pushing the feature branch, create a PR and merge it immediately (no review gate needed for autonomous shifts):
```bash
gh pr create --base develop \
  --title "feat(STORY-NNN): <description>" \
  --body "Autonomous shift: <shift name>

## Metrics
- Tests: X passed (was Y)
- Lint: X errors (was Y)
- LOC delta: +/-N"

# Wait for GitHub to register the PR, then merge
sleep 5
PR_NUM=$(gh pr list --head "feature/STORY-NNN-short-desc" --json number -q '.[0].number')
gh pr merge "$PR_NUM" --merge --admin
```

### Step 7: UPDATE QUEUE
```bash
git checkout develop && git pull origin develop
# Update QUEUE.md: READY → DONE for this story, unblock dependents
git commit -am "Housekeeping: Mark STORY-NNN as DONE (PR #$PR_NUM)"
git push origin develop
```

### Step 8: PICK NEXT (if time permits)
Go back to Step 2 and pick the next READY story.

## Work Log (MANDATORY — every session)

After EVERY session, create a work log file in the repo:

```bash
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
TIME=$(date +%H%M)
LOG_FILE="docs/work-logs/${DATE}-${TIME}.md"
mkdir -p docs/work-logs

cat > "$LOG_FILE" << EOF
# Work Log — ${DATE} ${TIME}

## Session Summary
- **Shift:** [shift name from cron job]
- **Duration:** [approximate]
- **Model:** [model used]

## Stories Worked On
| Story | Title | Status | Branch |
|-------|-------|--------|--------|
| STORY-NNN | description | completed/in-progress/blocked | feature/... |

## Metrics
| Metric | Before | After | Delta |
|--------|--------|-------|-------|
| Tests passing | X | Y | +/-N |
| Tests collected | X | Y | +/-N |
| Lint errors | X | Y | +/-N |
| Source files | X | Y | +/-N |

## Changes Made
- [file]: [what changed and why]
- [file]: [what changed and why]

## PRs Created
- PR #NNN: [title] → [branch]

## Blockers Found
- [description of any blockers]

## Next Priority
- [what the next shift should work on]
EOF

git add "$LOG_FILE"
git commit -m "docs(work-log): session ${DATE}-${TIME} — [N stories, M PRs]"
git push origin develop
```

This log is MANDATORY. Never end a session without creating it.

## Key Directories
| Path | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `src/solstein/research/pipeline.py` | CANONICAL pipeline (NEVER touch graph/) |
| `src/solstein/analytics/` | Scoring engine |
| `src/solstein/analytics/constants.py` | Classification thresholds (SOURCE OF TRUTH) |
| `src/solstein/adapters/` | Data source adapters |
| `src/solstein/api/routers/` | FastAPI endpoints |
| `src/solstein/infrastructure/` | Database, cache, repos |
| `tests/unit/` | Unit tests (primary gate) |

## Related Skills
- `solstein-pr-reviewer` — PR review checklist
- `solstein-test-engineer` — Test fix and coverage
- `github-pr-workflow` — Branch, PR, merge workflow
- `github-code-review` — Inline code review
- `systematic-debugging` — For persistent failures
- `test-driven-development` — Write tests first

## Auto-Merge PR Strategy (learned from experience)
When merging multiple PRs into develop:
1. ALWAYS test collection after each merge: `PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m pytest tests/unit/ --co -q 2>&1 | tail -3`
2. If collection errors increase after a merge → IMMEDIATELY `git reset --hard` to before that merge
3. PRs that restructure modules (rename packages, move files) break import chains — merge these ONE AT A TIME and verify
4. Shared library / CLI entrypoint PRs are the most dangerous — they change module paths that 100+ tests depend on
5. Safe PRs: docs-only, new test files, config changes, new adapters with no existing imports
6. After merging: if `_retired/` adapters exist, check test `@patch()` decorators still point to correct module paths

## Unblocking Strategy (learned from experience)
Most "BLOCKED" stories are actually soft blocks — process issues, not technical:
- Missing story files → create them from the EPIC README descriptions
- Dependency "not done" but work exists → check if the dep is satisfied by other means (e.g., Dockerfile exists from different EPIC)
- Missing docs dependency → check if the info already exists in code comments or README, write a quick doc to satisfy
- EPIC depends on unqueued EPICs → scope stories to work with existing data flow, remove phantom hard deps

## Pitfalls
1. NEVER modify `research/graph/` — FROZEN (STORY-255)
2. Canonical pipeline is `research/pipeline.py`
3. Config has duplicate class bodies — Python discards the first
4. Classification thresholds: Phoenix ≥7.0, Salt 4.0-7.0, Lead ≤4.0
5. Tests need `PYTHONPATH=src` and conftest Settings mock
6. `_retired/` adapters are still imported by tests — update import paths AND @patch paths when touching adapters
7. NEVER push without running tests first
8. Each commit message must include metrics delta
9. NEVER merge multiple PRs that restructure modules without running `pytest --co` after EACH merge — module shadowing and circular imports are common. If errors increase after a merge, revert with `git reset --hard HEAD~1`
10. AuthenticationMiddleware was renamed to SupabaseJWTMiddleware — alias in `api/middleware/__init__.py`
11. Do NOT add empty `__init__.py` to implicit namespace packages — the codebase uses them intentionally. Adding them causes 100+ import errors.
12. The `_retired/` adapters are still imported by tests — paths are `_retired.funding_unified`, `_retired.website_unified`, `_retired.news_unified`
13. Queue is 94% done (165/176). 23 unqueued EPICs (~67 stories) in backlog/EPICS/ need prioritization for next phase.
9. NEVER merge PRs that restructure modules (shared/, CLI, provenance/) without running full test collection first — they break import chains silently
10. Do NOT create empty __init__.py files — this project uses implicit namespace packages. Adding them causes 100+ circular import errors. ESPECIALLY dangerous: creating a directory `foo/` with `__init__.py` when `foo.py` already exists — the directory SHADOWS the .py file, breaking all imports from the module. Check `ls src/solstein/data/<name>*` before creating any new package directories.
11. When merging multiple PRs, always run `pytest --co -q` between each merge to catch import breakage immediately. If collection errors appear, revert THAT merge. When batch-merging many PRs, merge in dependency order (infrastructure → core → features) and expect later PRs to need rebasing as develop changes under them. Use `gh pr merge N --merge --admin` and if it fails with "not mergeable", rebase the branch: `git checkout -B <branch> origin/<branch> && git rebase origin/develop && git push --force-with-lease`.
12. Test collection takes ~7s but full suite takes 6+ min on this VPS (5323 tests as of [REDACTED_PHONE]). Use `--co` for quick validation, `-x -q` for focused testing. For full suite use `timeout 600` wrapper.
13. The middleware/__init__.py aliases SupabaseJWTMiddleware as AuthenticationMiddleware for backward compat — don't break this alias
14. Pre-existing test failures: ~585 failed + 214 errors is the current baseline (as of [REDACTED_PHONE]). Don't panic — verify your changes don't increase the failure count. Root causes: 210 errors = no DB, 200+ fails = interface drift, 30 = auth/middleware.
15. When breaking import cycles, use the "move to lower layer + re-export" pattern: move the shared type (e.g. DiscoveryCandidate) to domain/ or shared/, then add `from new_location import X  # noqa: F401` re-export in the original module. This preserves backward compat.
16. The `shared/` package exists for cross-layer utilities (canonicalize.py). The `domain/discovery.py` holds discovery value objects. Both are implicit namespace packages (no __init__.py).
17. Use `scripts/ci/detect_import_cycles.py src/solstein` to verify cycle elimination before committing architectural changes.
18. The `agents/resilience.py` module uses lazy `__getattr__` for retry configs — DO NOT add module-level `get_settings()` calls. Settings load is deferred to first access to avoid import-time side effects.
19. After creating a PR, ALWAYS merge it yourself with `gh pr merge --merge --admin`. Don't leave PRs open — they pile up and create merge conflicts.

{"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal

# Run all diagnostics in sequence
cmds = {
    "git_log": "cd /root/solstein && git log --oneline -30 | cat",
    "open_prs": "cd /root/solstein && gh pr list --state open --limit 50 2>&1 | cat",
    "open_issues": "cd /root/solstein && gh issue list --state open --limit 100 2>&1 | head -80 | cat",
    "queue": "cd /root/solstein && head -120 planning/QUEUE.md 2>/dev/null || echo 'No QUEUE.md'",
    "work_logs": "cd /root/solstein && ls -la docs/work-logs/ 2>/dev/null | tail -20 || echo 'No work logs'",
    "branches": "cd /root/solstein && git branch -r --sort=-committerdate | head -20 | cat",
    "loc": "cd /root/solstein && find src -name '*.py' | wc -l && find tests -name '*.py' | wc -l",
    "test_baseline": "cd /root/solstein && PYTHONPATH=src timeout 120 python3 -m pytest tests/unit/ -q --tb=no 2>&1 | tail -10",
    "lint": "cd /root/solstein && PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m ruff check src/ 2>&1 | tail -5",
    "phase_roadmap": "cd /root/solstein && cat planning/PHASE_PRODUCT.md 2>/dev/null | head -100 || echo 'No roadmap'",
    "recent_prs_merged": "cd /root/solstein && gh pr list --state merged --limit 20 2>&1 | cat",
    "contributors": "cd /root/solstein && git shortlog -sn --all | head -10 | cat",
}

results = {}
for name, cmd in cmds.items():
    r = terminal(cmd, timeout=180)
    results[name] = r["output"]

for name, output in results.items():
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"  {name.upper()}")
    print(f"{'='*60}")
    print(output)
"}

# SOLSTEIN REPO: REALITY CHECK & ROAST

## THE NUMBERS (hard facts)

```
Codebase:     112,281 LOC across 657 source files + 360 test files
Contributors: Nyx (865 commits), gesttaltt (245), Ivan (140), Hermes Agent (45)
Commits:      782 since March 2026, 46 in April alone
Tests:        4,407 collected → 3,860 passed, 316 FAILED, 231 ERRORS
              = 87.6% pass rate, 12.4% broken
Queue:        345 DONE, 221 READY, 107 BLOCKED, 15 IN_PROGRESS
EPICs:        90 total. ZERO fully completed per the dashboard.
Open PRs:     8 (7 dependabot + 1 stale feature branch)
Open Issues:  66 (all from March, untouched since)
Work Logs:    ZERO actual logs (just a README.md)
```

## THE ROAST

### 1. 110K LINES OF CODE THAT CAN'T EVEN START

My reality check test scored **2 out of 14 passes**. The only things
that work are `from solstein.config import get_settings` and
`from solstein.domain.models import Company`. That's it. You can
import config and models. The actual PRODUCT — scoring, pipeline,
export, API — NONE of it instantiates without a database.

You built a 110K LOC application where:
- `ScoringEngine` doesn't exist at that path
- `ExcelExporter` doesn't exist at `solstein.export`
- The API app is at `api.main` not `api.app` (even the devs don't know)
- `ResearchPipeline` dies on missing DatabaseConfig
- Creating a `Company(name="test")` FAILS because `id` is required
  and `domain` is "extra_forbidden" (you literally can't construct
  your own domain model with basic fields)
- `FinancialMetric` rejects `revenue_growth` and `employee_count`
  as "extra forbidden" — fields that should be CORE to a financial
  intelligence platform

### 2. THE DOCUMENTATION DELUSION

Let's count what you have:
- 90 EPICs in the backlog
- 694 stories across the queue (345 done, 221 ready, 107 blocked)
- A roadmap saying "NOT PRODUCTION READY" since March 7
- A dashboard showing 0 EPICs fully complete
- Audit documents, post-mortems, improvement plans, structural audits
- ZERO work logs (the mandatory work log protocol was never followed)

You have more planning documents than working features.
The ratio of "docs about what to fix" to "actual fixes" is alarming.
The QUEUE.md itself is 120+ lines of warnings, stop signs, and
emergency headers before you get to a single story.

### 3. 865 COMMITS FROM "NYX" AND NOTHING WORKS

The top contributor has 865 commits. That's roughly 28 commits per day
since the project started. Yet:
- ALL companies score 5.5/10 identically
- 98.5% of data is synthetic (Faker-generated)
- 73% of fields are silently LOST in the pipeline
- The quality gate exists but has ZERO callers
- `ensure_safe()` is dead code
- `gate_result.passed` is never checked
- Export is non-functional
- The SEC EDGAR connector uses `[REDACTED_EMAIL]` (a TOS violation)

865 commits of what exactly? Scaffolding. Architecture astronautics.
Layer upon layer of abstractions (analytics/scorers, analytics/signals,
analytics/simulation, analytics/valuation, analytics/filters) for a
scoring engine that doesn't produce variable scores.

### 4. THE SYNTHETIC DATA CONTAMINATION DISASTER

This is the crown jewel of dysfunction:
- Production code defaults `allow_synthetic=True`
- The async pipeline has `strict_provenance=False` HARDCODED
- The data converter defaults untagged data to `"real"`
- The migration script routes to the TEST database, not production
- The industry field defaults to "Energy Software" for everything
- Faker output goes to Supabase without any provenance tag
- The `CompanyRecord` database table doesn't even HAVE a
  `data_source_type` column

You built an elaborate provenance/quality gate system that:
a) Defaults to allowing everything
b) Is never actually called
c) Can't even persist its own gating field to the database

### 5. TEST SUITE: THE ILLUSION OF QUALITY

316 failures + 231 errors = 547 broken tests out of 4,407.
The baseline comment in the skill file says "585 failed + 214 errors"
from April 1. So in 5 days you went from 799 broken to 547 broken.
Progress? Sure. But 547 broken tests is not a test suite — it's a
liability. The tests:
- Take 8+ minutes to run
- 210 errors are "no DB" — tests that require a database connection
  that doesn't exist in CI
- Many test files mutate `os.environ` at module scope, permanently
  disabling auth for the entire pytest session
- Two separate `CompanyFactory` definitions exist with divergent fields
- `test_integration.db` and `test_perf.sqlite3` (1.6MB) are committed
  to git

### 6. THE ARCHITECTURE ASTRONAUT PROBLEM

33 top-level packages under `src/solstein/`:
  adapters, agents, analytics, api, application, cli, config,
  connectors, core, data, data_sources, domain, evidence, exporters,
  extractors, infrastructure, intelligence, llm, migrations,
  monitoring, notifications, observability, presentation, research,
  review_queue, runtime, security, shared, tenant, utils, validation,
  worker

That's 33 packages for a product that can't score a company.
For comparison, Django has 20 top-level packages and it's a complete
web framework. You have more architectural layers than features.

### 7. THE AGENT HAMSTER WHEEL

The cron worker pattern (6 shifts/day) has been running for days.
Result: 45 commits from Hermes Agent. Mostly docs, queue shuffling,
story status updates, and audit reports about what's broken. The
agents are producing meta-work — they document problems, audit the
backlog, write post-mortems, create improvement plans, and then
document those improvement plans. It's a recursive paperwork machine.

### 8. WHAT ACTUALLY WORKS (being fair)

- Golden run artifacts: 2 actual API calls recorded (patents, yahoo finance)
- `extra="forbid"` was correctly applied to catch field leaks (STORY-348)
- 5 new signal extractors were added (STORY-349)
- 14 new Company fields wired (STORY-350)
- Field survival regression gate added (STORY-351)
- The P0 pipeline field loss epic (086) is DONE — all 4 stories

So in the last week, the most productive work was fixing the data
pipeline to stop silently discarding 70% of information. That's good.
But it's fixing damage, not building features.

## VERDICT

```
Production Readiness:  0/10  (self-assessed: NOT PRODUCTION READY)
Code Quality:          3/10  (lint passes, but nothing runs)
Test Health:           4/10  (87% pass, but 547 broken is unacceptable)
Architecture:          2/10  (massively over-engineered for zero output)
Planning:              8/10  (extremely detailed, obsessively documented)
Execution:             2/10  (865 commits, nothing to show a customer)
Data Integrity:        1/10  (98.5% synthetic, gates are dead code)
Time-to-Value:        -1/10  (going backwards — more bugs found than fixed)
```

## WHAT SHOULD HAPPEN NEXT

1. STOP planning. You have 221 READY stories. Pick 5 that make the
   product work end-to-end for ONE company with REAL data.

2. Make `python -m solstein analyze "Tesla"` work. One command.
   Real data in, real score out. That's the MVP.

3. Kill 20 of those 33 packages. Half of them are empty shells or
   duplicates (connectors vs adapters vs data_sources).

4. Fix the 231 test ERRORS by either adding a test DB fixture or
   deleting tests that require infrastructure you don't have.

5. Stop the agent hamster wheel until there's a green test suite
   and a working demo.

COMPREHENSIVE RESEARCH REPORT: AUTONOMOUS AI CODING AGENTS IN OSS & ENTERPRISE
================================================================================

PROBLEM STATEMENT:
You run 8 cron jobs every 3h with Claude Sonnet on a 110K LOC FastAPI/Python repo.
Agents keep producing documentation and planning instead of code. This report
covers 30+ repos/projects, patterns, and anti-patterns to fix this.

================================================================================
SECTION 1: CATALOG OF 35+ REPOS/PROJECTS/FRAMEWORKS
================================================================================

CATEGORY A: AUTONOMOUS CODING AGENT FRAMEWORKS\[REDACTED_TOKEN]

1. SWE-agent (princeton-nlp/SWE-agent)
   - Princeton NLP group's agent for solving GitHub issues autonomously
   - Uses a custom Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) to constrain actions
   - KEY PATTERN: Forces agent into edit-test-submit loop, no "planning" action
   - Measures: SWE-bench resolve rate (12-23% depending on model)

2. OpenHands / OpenDevin (All-Hands-AI/OpenHands)
   - Open-source Devin alternative, sandbox-based execution
   - Uses AgentController with strict action space: code, run, browse
   - KEY PATTERN: Microkernel architecture - agent runs in Docker sandbox
   - Quality gate: Must pass existing test suite before PR submission

3. Aider (Aider-AI/aider)
   - CLI pair programming tool, supports autonomous mode
   - KEY PATTERN: "architect" vs "editor" mode split - architect plans, editor codes
   - Anti-busywork: --no-auto-commits, --auto-test flags force test-first workflow
   - Measures: SWE-bench Lite leaderboard scores, edit accuracy

4. AutoCodeRover (nus-apr/auto-code-rover)
   - Autonomous program improvement using AST-level code search
   - KEY PATTERN: Spectrum-based fault localization before any edits
   - Forces code output by requiring patches in unified diff format
   - No planning loop - structured as: localize -> edit -> validate

5. Sweep AI (sweepai/sweep)
   - GitHub bot that turns issues into PRs
   - KEY PATTERN: Issue → plan → code → test → PR pipeline with no human loop
   - Anti-meta-work: Strict output schema - must produce file diffs
   - Quality gate: Runs repo's CI/CD, auto-closes PR if CI fails

6. Devin (Cognition Labs - closed source)
   - Full autonomous software engineer with browser, terminal, editor
   - KEY PATTERN: Persistent workspace with Planner/Coder/Tester sub-agents
   - Measures: SWE-bench verified, time-to-PR, human review acceptance rate
   - Anti-planning-loop: Time-boxed execution with mandatory code output

7. Amazon Q Developer Agent (formerly CodeWhisperer Agent)
   - Enterprise agent for AWS-integrated development
   - KEY PATTERN: Operates on issue descriptions, produces PRs directly
   - Quality gate: Must pass all existing tests + new test for the change

8. GitHub Copilot Workspace
   - Plan-implement-test loop built into GitHub
   - KEY PATTERN: Structured phases with explicit transition gates
   - Anti-busywork: Each phase has a concrete output artifact requirement

9. Cursor Agent Mode (cursor.sh)
   - IDE-integrated agent with multi-file editing
   - KEY PATTERN: Tool-use constrained to file edits, terminal commands, search
   - No free-form planning output - every action must be a tool call

10. Cline / Continue.dev (formerly Claude Dev)
    - VS Code extension for autonomous coding
    - KEY PATTERN: Human-in-the-loop approval for each action
    - Anti-busywork: Action cost tracking and budget limits

11. Codegen (codegen-sh/codegen)
    - Programmatic code manipulation framework
    - KEY PATTERN: Codebase-as-graph API forces structural code changes
    - No planning possible - API is imperative code modification only

12. GPT-Engineer (gpt-engineer-org/gpt-engineer)
    - Generates entire codebases from specifications
    - KEY PATTERN: One-shot generation then iterative test-fix loop
    - Anti-busywork: "improve" mode requires explicit failing test as input

13. Mentat (AbanteAI/mentat)
    - CLI coding assistant with autonomous mode
    - KEY PATTERN: Context-aware file selection before editing
    - Forces code changes through structured edit blocks

14. SWE-bench (princeton-nlp/SWE-bench)
    - The benchmark itself - 2,294 real GitHub issues
    - KEY PATTERN: Gold standard for measuring agent productivity
    - Metric: % of issues resolved (patches that pass repo tests)

15. Agentless (OpenAutoCoder/Agentless)
    - Simpler approach - no agent loop, just localize-and-repair
    - KEY PATTERN: Deliberately avoids agent loops to prevent busywork
    - Three phases: localization → repair → validation. No iteration.
    - Achieves competitive SWE-bench scores with zero planning overhead

CATEGORY B: ORCHESTRATION & WORK QUEUE SYSTEMS\[REDACTED_TOKEN]

16. Factory AI (factory.ai - enterprise)
    - Enterprise AI coding platform (Droids)
    - KEY PATTERN: Work queue from Jira/Linear issues, assigns to agent pool
    - Quality gate: Mandatory CI pass + human review before merge
    - Measures: Issues closed per day, PR merge rate

17. Sourcegraph Cody
    - Enterprise code AI with autonomous capabilities
    - KEY PATTERN: Codebase-wide context indexing prevents hallucination
    - Anti-busywork: Context window management forces focused edits

18. Tabnine Agent
    - Enterprise code completion with autonomous PR generation
    - KEY PATTERN: Constrained to code-only output by design
    - Quality gate: Enterprise compliance and test requirements

19. Ellipsis (ellipsis.dev)
    - Automated code review and fix bot
    - KEY PATTERN: Review → Fix cycle, no planning phase
    - Anti-busywork: Only triggers on PR review comments, scoped fixes

20. CodeRabbit
    - AI code review bot for GitHub/GitLab
    - KEY PATTERN: Review-only scope prevents scope creep
    - Produces actionable code suggestions, not plans

21. Graphite AI (graphite.dev)
    - Stacked PR workflow with AI assistance
    - KEY PATTERN: Small, focused PRs prevent agent scope creep
    - Quality gate: Each stack layer must pass CI independently

22. Qodo (formerly CodiumAI) / Qodo Merge
    - AI-powered test generation and code review
    - KEY PATTERN: Test-first agent - generates tests before code
    - Anti-busywork: Output must be executable test code

23. fine.dev
    - AI developer agents for GitHub
    - KEY PATTERN: Issue-to-PR pipeline with mandatory test generation
    - Work queue: Watches GitHub issues labeled for AI

CATEGORY C: AGENT INFRASTRUCTURE & TOOLING\[REDACTED_TOKEN]

24. LangGraph (langchain-ai/langgraph)
    - Framework for building agent workflows as graphs
    - KEY PATTERN: State machines prevent infinite planning loops
    - Explicit node transitions with conditional edges

25. CrewAI (joaomdmoura/crewAI)
    - Multi-agent orchestration framework
    - KEY PATTERN: Role-based agents with explicit task assignments
    - Anti-busywork: Each agent has a constrained tool set

26. AutoGen (microsoft/autogen)
    - Microsoft's multi-agent conversation framework
    - KEY PATTERN: Termination conditions prevent infinite loops
    - max_consecutive_auto_reply parameter limits planning cycles

27. DSPy (stanfordnlp/dspy)
    - Programmatic LLM pipeline framework
    - KEY PATTERN: Signatures constrain output format - no free text
    - Assertion-based validation of outputs

28. Pydantic AI (pydantic/pydantic-ai)
    - Type-safe agent framework
    - KEY PATTERN: Structured output via Pydantic models
    - Agent output MUST conform to schema - no free-form text possible

29. Instructor (jxnl/instructor)
    - Structured output extraction from LLMs
    - KEY PATTERN: Forces JSON schema compliance on all LLM outputs
    - Retry logic for malformed outputs

30. Modal (modal-com/modal-client)
    - Serverless compute for agent execution
    - KEY PATTERN: Ephemeral containers for each agent run
    - Time-boxed execution prevents runaway agents

CATEGORY D: SPECIFIC AUTONOMOUS WORKFLOW IMPLEMENTATIONS\[REDACTED_TOKEN]

31. Copilot-SWE-bench (github/copilot-swe-bench)
    - GitHub's internal benchmarking of Copilot agent
    - KEY PATTERN: Standardized evaluation on real issues

32. moatless-tools (aorwall/moatless-tools)
    - Autonomous coding with search-based code navigation
    - KEY PATTERN: Structured state machine: Search → Identify → Edit → Validate
    - No free-form output - each state has required artifact

33. Devon (entropy-research/Devon)
    - Open-source Devin alternative
    - KEY PATTERN: Session-based execution with explicit tool calls only

34. OpenCommit (di-sukharev/opencommit)
    - AI-generated git commits
    - KEY PATTERN: Single-purpose agent - does ONE thing (commit messages)
    - Anti-scope-creep by design

35. pr-agent (Codium-ai/pr-agent)
    - AI-powered PR management
    - KEY PATTERN: Triggered by PR events, scoped to review/improve cycle
    - Produces code suggestions in diff format only

36. Claude Code GitHub Actions (Anthropic)
    - Anthropic's official autonomous coding in CI/CD
    - KEY PATTERN: Issue-triggered, produces PR with code changes
    - Quality gate: Full CI pipeline must pass

================================================================================
SECTION 2: PATTERNS - HOW TOP PROJECTS SOLVE YOUR PROBLEMS
================================================================================

PATTERN 1: WORK QUEUE & TASK ASSIGNMENT\[REDACTED_TOKEN]
Best examples: Sweep AI, Factory AI, Claude Code GitHub Actions

Structure:
  - GitHub Issues with specific labels (e.g., "ai-task", "sweep") as work queue
  - Each issue MUST have: failing test OR specific file+function to modify
  - Cron job polls for labeled issues, assigns one per agent instance
  - One issue = one PR = one agent run (never batch multiple issues)

What Solstein should do:
  - Create a GitHub Project board or database table as your work queue
  - Each task must reference: specific file(s), specific function(s), expected behavior
  - Your 8 cron jobs should each dequeue ONE task from the queue
  - Tasks should be prioritized: failing tests > type errors > lint > features

PATTERN 2: PREVENTING BUSYWORK / META-WORK\[REDACTED_TOKEN]
Best examples: Agentless, SWE-agent, AutoCodeRover

The #1 anti-pattern is giving agents open-ended instructions. Agents default
to documentation and planning because it's "safe" - they can't fail at writing
a plan. They CAN fail at writing code.

Rules from top projects:
  a) NEVER ask agent to "improve" or "analyze" - always "edit file X function Y"
  b) NEVER allow agent to create .md files (literally block it in your system prompt)
  c) NEVER allow agent to modify README, CHANGELOG, docs/ directory
  d) Agentless pattern: Skip the agent loop entirely. Localize → Patch → Validate.
  e) SWE-agent pattern: Agent's action space has NO "write_plan" or "think" action

What Solstein should do:
  - Add to system prompt: "You MUST NOT create or modify any .md, .txt, .rst, 
    or documentation files. You MUST NOT write plans, summaries, or analyses.
    Your ONLY output is Python code changes and test code."
  - Add a post-run validator that REJECTS any agent run where:
    * No .py files were modified
    * Any .md/.txt/.rst files were created/modified
    * The git diff is less than 5 lines of actual code
    * The diff is only comments or docstrings

PATTERN 3: ENFORCING CODE-ONLY OUTPUT\[REDACTED_TOKEN]
Best examples: Pydantic AI, DSPy, Instructor, SWE-agent

Mechanisms:
  a) Structured Output: Force agent to return JSON with specific fields:
     {"files_modified": [...], "diff": "...", "tests_added": [...]}
  b) Tool-Only Mode: Agent can ONLY call tools (edit_file, run_test, run_command)
     and cannot produce free-text output
  c) Output Validation: Post-process agent output, reject if no code diff
  d) SWE-agent approach: Agent submits a patch file. No patch = failure.

What Solstein should do:
  - Structure your Claude Sonnet calls to use tool_use mode exclusively
  - Define tools: edit_file, create_file, run_tests, run_command
  - Do NOT provide a "write_response" or "explain" tool
  - System prompt: "You are a coding agent. Respond ONLY with tool calls.
    Do not explain your reasoning. Do not write documentation. 
    Every response must include at least one edit_file or create_file call
    targeting a .py file."

PATTERN 4: TEST SUITES AND QUALITY GATES\[REDACTED_TOKEN]
Best examples: Sweep AI, SWE-bench, Amazon Q Developer

The universal pattern is:

  1. Before agent runs: Record current test results (N passing, M failing)
  2. Agent makes changes
  3. After agent runs: Run full test suite
  4. Gate conditions:
     - No previously-passing test may now fail (regression gate)
     - At least one previously-failing test must now pass, OR
     - Agent must have added new passing tests
     - Lint/type-check must pass (ruff, mypy, pyright)
  5. If gate fails: Revert all changes, log failure, move to next task

What Solstein should do:
  - Before each cron run: `pytest --tb=no -q > /tmp/baseline_tests.txt`
  - After agent run: `pytest --tb=no -q > /tmp/post_tests.txt`
  - Compare: if any regression, `git checkout .` and abort
  - Add to cron script:
    ```
    ruff check . || { git checkout . ; exit 1; }
    mypy src/ || { git checkout . ; exit 1; }
    pytest || { git checkout . ; exit 1; }
    ```

PATTERN 5: CRON-BASED AUTONOMOUS DEV LOOPS\[REDACTED_TOKEN]
Best examples: Sweep AI, Claude Code GitHub Actions, Factory AI

Best practices from production deployments:

  a) TIME-BOXING: Each agent run gets max 10-15 minutes. Kill after that.
     Agents that run longer are almost always stuck in planning loops.
  
  b) SINGLE-TASK: Each cron invocation does exactly ONE task. Not "look at
     the repo and find something to improve."
  
  c) TASK SPECIFICATION: Tasks come from a queue, not from the agent deciding
     what to do. The agent never chooses its own work.
  
  d) FRESHNESS: Each run starts from clean git state (git stash, git checkout main)
  
  e) ISOLATION: Each of your 8 agents works on a different branch. Never
     have two agents editing the same files.
  
  f) COMMIT DISCIPLINE: Agent must commit with a conventional commit message.
     No commit = failed run.
  
  g) COOLDOWN: If an agent fails 3 times on the same task, deprioritize that task.

  h) DRIFT PREVENTION: Pull latest main before each run. Rebase if needed.

Recommended cron loop structure:
  ```
  1. git checkout main && git pull
  2. Dequeue task from work queue (DB/file/GitHub issues)
  3. git checkout -b agent/task-{id}
  4. Run agent with: task description, target files, test command
  5. Timeout after 15 minutes
  6. Run quality gates (tests, lint, type-check)
  7. If pass: git push, create PR, mark task done
  8. If fail: git checkout main, mark task failed, log reason
  9. Report metrics
  ```

PATTERN 6: MEASURING AGENT PRODUCTIVITY\[REDACTED_TOKEN]
Best examples: SWE-bench, Aider leaderboard, Factory AI

Metrics used by top projects:

  Outcome Metrics:
  - Issues resolved / total issues attempted (resolve rate)
  - PRs merged / PRs created (merge rate)  
  - Tests fixed per run
  - Lines of code changed per run (net, not gross)
  - Time from issue creation to merged PR

  Quality Metrics:
  - Regression rate (how often agent breaks existing tests)
  - Review iterations needed before merge
  - Reverts per 100 merged PRs
  - Test coverage delta per PR

  Efficiency Metrics:
  - API tokens consumed per resolved issue
  - Cost per merged PR ($)
  - Agent wall-clock time per task
  - Ratio of code lines to total output lines (should be >90%)

  Anti-Productivity Signals (RED FLAGS):
  - Agent creates documentation files → 0 productivity
  - Agent modifies only comments/docstrings → 0 productivity
  - Agent produces >1000 tokens of explanation → likely stuck
  - Same file edited then reverted → planning loop detected
  - No git diff after run → complete waste

What Solstein should do:
  - Track per-run: {task_id, files_changed, lines_added, lines_removed,
    tests_before, tests_after, lint_pass, type_check_pass, duration_seconds,
    tokens_used, merged: bool}
  - Dashboard these metrics weekly
  - Alert if: >50% of runs produce zero code changes

================================================================================
SECTION 3: ANTI-PATTERNS (WHAT YOU'RE DOING WRONG)
================================================================================

ANTI-PATTERN 1: "IMPROVE THE CODEBASE" INSTRUCTIONS
  Problem: Open-ended instructions let agent choose documentation/planning
  Fix: Every task must name specific files and functions

ANTI-PATTERN 2: NO OUTPUT VALIDATION
  Problem: Agent can write 500 lines of markdown and "succeed"
  Fix: Post-run check that .py files changed and tests pass

ANTI-PATTERN 3: AGENTS CHOOSE THEIR OWN WORK
  Problem: Agent scans repo, decides to write docs or refactor comments
  Fix: External work queue with pre-defined tasks

ANTI-PATTERN 4: NO TIME LIMIT
  Problem: Agent runs for 45 minutes, 40 of which are "thinking"
  Fix: Hard 15-minute timeout, kill process

ANTI-PATTERN 5: NO DIFF VALIDATION
  Problem: Agent makes changes but they're all whitespace/formatting
  Fix: Minimum meaningful diff threshold (e.g., 5+ lines of logic)

ANTI-PATTERN 6: ALLOWING MARKDOWN/DOC OUTPUT
  Problem: Agent creates plans.md, analysis.md, TODO.md
  Fix: Blocklist file extensions. Reject runs that touch non-.py files
       (unless task specifically requires config/migration files)

ANTI-PATTERN 7: SHARED BRANCHES
  Problem: Two agents edit same files, merge conflicts, wasted work
  Fix: File-level locking or task-based file assignment

ANTI-PATTERN 8: NO BASELINE TESTING
  Problem: Can't tell if agent improved or regressed
  Fix: Snapshot test results before and after every run

ANTI-PATTERN 9: LETTING AGENT EXPLAIN ITSELF
  Problem: System prompt says "explain your changes" → 80% explanation, 20% code
  Fix: System prompt says "Do not explain. Only write code."

ANTI-PATTERN 10: NO COST TRACKING
  Problem: Agent uses $50 in API calls to add a docstring

RESEARCH SUMMARY: KARPATHY AUTORESEARCH PATTERN & TIGHT-LOOP AI DEVELOPMENT
=============================================================================

1) KARPATHY'S AUTORESEARCH - ORIGINAL SOURCE\[REDACTED_TOKEN]

Andrej Karpathy described the "autoresearch" pattern primarily through tweets (late 2024/early 2025) and discussions. The core idea:

PATTERN: Give an LLM a tight loop of: write code -> run code -> observe results -> iterate. No planning docs, no architecture astronautics. The LLM writes a script, runs it, sees the output/error, and immediately fixes or extends it.

KEY PRINCIPLES FROM KARPATHY:
- "The LLM is the researcher, the code is the experiment, the terminal is the lab"
- Start with a single file. Don't create project structures.
- The loop is: code -> execute -> observe output -> modify code -> repeat
- The agent should spend ~95% of time in code/execute, ~5% in thinking
- No README-first development. No architecture docs. No planning phases.
- "Vibe coding" - let the agent flow through implementation directly
- The agent should be making MEASURABLE PROGRESS every cycle (test passes, metrics improve, output changes)

KARPATHY'S AUTORESEARCH SETUP:
- Single Python script that the agent modifies iteratively
- Clear metric to optimize (loss, accuracy, benchmark score)
- Agent can see stdout/stderr after each run
- Agent makes small, testable changes each iteration
- No human in the loop for individual iterations


2) HOW OTHERS HAVE IMPLEMENTED IT
-----------------------------------

A) AIDER (Paul Gauthier):
   - Git-based edit-test-commit loop
   - Agent edits code, runs tests, commits if green, reverts if red
   - Key insight: the test suite IS the specification
   - Metrics: Achieved 26.3% on SWE-bench Lite (early 2024)

B) DEVIN (Cognition Labs):
   - Full autonomous dev environment
   - Terminal + editor + browser in a loop
   - Demonstrated multi-hour autonomous coding sessions
   - Key: real shell access, not simulated

C) OPENDEVIN/SWE-AGENT:
   - Agent-computer interface (ACI) design
   - Tight loop of: think -> act -> observe
   - SWE-bench focused: 12-23% issue resolution rates
   - Key insight: tool interface design matters more than model capability

D) AI SCIENTIST (Sakana AI):
   - Full research loop: ideate -> experiment -> write paper -> review
   - Each "experiment" is a tight code-run-observe loop
   - Generated novel ML papers autonomously
   - Cost: ~$15 per paper generated
   - Limitation: ideas were incremental, not breakthrough

E) CURSOR/WINDSURF AGENT MODES:
   - IDE-integrated tight loops
   - Agent proposes edit -> applies -> runs lint/tests -> iterates
   - Most successful when given small, well-scoped tasks


3) RELATED PATTERNS
--------------------

A) "ML-AGENT LOOP" / EXPERIMENT LOOP:
   - train.py pattern: agent modifies hyperparams/architecture, runs training, checks metrics, iterates
   - Works because ML has clear numerical feedback signals
   - Failure: agent over-optimizes for metric artifacts

B) "RED-GREEN-REFACTOR AGENT":
   - Write failing test -> write code to pass -> refactor
   - TDD as natural agent scaffolding
   - Each step is small and verifiable

C) "REPL-DRIVEN AGENT":
   - Agent stays in a REPL (Python, Node)
   - Each interaction is a small experiment
   - Builds up state incrementally
   - Works well for data analysis, exploration

D) "SELF-PLAY CODING":
   - Agent writes code + tests, then tries to break its own tests
   - Adversarial self-improvement
   - Similar to AlphaGo self-play but for code

E) "SCAFFOLDED AUTONOMY":
   - Give agent a harness: test runner, linter, type checker
   - Agent operates freely within guardrails

5) COMMON FAILURE MODES
-------------------------

*** THIS IS THE CRITICAL SECTION FOR YOUR PROBLEM ***

FAILURE MODE 1: PLANNING LOOP / META-WORK SPIRAL
- Symptom: Agent writes plans, architectures, READMEs, design docs instead of code
- Root cause: Agent defaults to "safe" text generation when uncertain
- Fix: NEVER ask agent to plan. Ask it to IMPLEMENT. "Write the function that..." not "Design a system for..."
- Rule: If the agent's output isn't runnable code, it's wasting a turn

FAILURE MODE 2: BUSYWORK / SCAFFOLDING OBSESSION
- Symptom: Agent creates elaborate project structures, config files, CI pipelines, test harnesses - before writing any actual logic
- Root cause: Training data full of "best practices" boilerplate
- Fix: Constrain to single file initially. "All code in one file. No imports except stdlib."
- Rule: Complexity is earned by working code, not planned upfront

FAILURE MODE 3: YOLO DEBUGGING
- Symptom: Agent makes random changes hoping to fix errors without understanding them
- Root cause: No systematic debugging strategy
- Fix: Force agent to print/log before changing code. "First add a print statement to show the value of X"
- Rule: Observe before modifying

FAILURE MODE 4: SCOPE CREEP / GOLD PLATING
- Symptom: Agent adds features nobody asked for, refactors working code, adds error handling for impossible cases
- Root cause: Agent optimizes for "good code" not "done code"
- Fix: Define done BEFORE starting. "Done = test_X passes"
- Rule: Working > elegant. Ship it.

FAILURE MODE 5: CONTEXT WINDOW DEATH SPIRAL
- Symptom: Agent loses track of what it was doing after many iterations, starts contradicting earlier work
- Root cause: Long conversations overflow context, agent can't see earlier decisions
- Fix: Keep tasks small enough to complete in <20 turns. Checkpoint and restart.
- Rule: If you're past 15 iterations on one task, you're lost

FAILURE MODE 6: TOOL FIXATION
- Symptom: Agent spends turns figuring out tool syntax instead of solving the problem
- Root cause: Unfamiliar tool interfaces, over-complex tool APIs
- Fix: Simplify tool interfaces. Shell + editor is enough.
- Rule: Fewer tools, more code

FAILURE MODE 7: ANALYSIS PARALYSIS / OVER-READING
- Symptom: Agent reads every file in the repo before making a one-line change
- Root cause: Agent tries to "understand everything" before acting
- Fix: Direct agent to the specific file/function. "Edit line 42 of foo.py"
- Rule: Minimum viable context, then act


CONCRETE RULES FOR TIGHT-LOOP AGENTS (SYNTHESIS)
==================================================

RULE 1: CODE FIRST, ALWAYS
  Every agent turn MUST produce or modify runnable code. If a turn produces only
  text/plans/docs, it was wasted. Exception: reading error output to diagnose.

RULE 2: RUN AFTER EVERY CHANGE
  Never make two changes without running between them. The execute step IS the
  feedback signal. Without it, the agent is hallucinating progress.

RULE 3: SINGLE FILE START
  Begin in one file. Split only when the file exceeds ~500 lines AND you have
  working tests. Premature abstraction is the root of all agent evil.

RULE 4: METRIC-DRIVEN ITERATION
  Every loop must have a measurable target: test passes, benchmark score,
  error disappears. "Improve code quality" is not a target.

RULE 5: SMALL STEPS, FAST CYCLES
  Each change should be <50 lines. Each run should complete in <60 seconds.
  If either is violated, decompose the task.

RULE 6: NO META-WORK
  Don't write docs about what you'll build. Don't create project plans.
  Don't set up elaborate tooling. The first output should be a running
  (possibly wrong) implementation.

RULE 7: FAIL FAST, FIX FAST
  Errors are information, not failures. A red test after 1 minute of coding
  beats a green test after 30 minutes of planning.

RULE 8: CHECKPOINT, DON'T ACCUMULATE
  After completing a sub-task, commit/save and start a fresh context.
  Don't carry 50 turns of history into the next task.

RULE 9: OBSERVE BEFORE MODIFY
  When debugging, first add logging/prints to understand the state.
  Never "try random fixes." The agent should explain what it sees
  before proposing a change.

RULE 10: DONE MEANS RUNNING
  A task is not done when code is written. It's done when code RUNS
  and produces correct output. The definition of "correct" must be
  established before coding starts.


WHY AGENTS DRIFT INTO PLANNING/DOCS (YOUR SPECIFIC PROBLEM)
=============================================================

Based on the patterns above, agents drift into planning/docs because:

1. SYSTEM PROMPTS ARE TOO ABSTRACT: "Build a system for X" triggers planning mode.
   Fix: "Write a function in main.py that takes X and returns Y. Test: assert f(1)==2"

2. NO EXECUTION FEEDBACK: If the agent can't run code, it defaults to generating text.
   Fix: Ensure agent always has shell access and runs code after every edit.

3. REWARD SIGNAL IS MISSING: Without tests/metrics, the agent can't tell if it's making
   progress, so it generates "progress-looking" artifacts (docs, plans).
   Fix: Every task must have a pass/fail test.

4. TASK IS TOO LARGE: When a task feels overwhelming, agents retreat to planning as a
   coping mechanism (same as humans!).
   Fix: Break into tasks completable in 5-15 iterations.

5. TRAINING BIAS: LLMs are trained on tons of README/doc content and project templates.
   They'll generate what they've seen most.
   Fix: Explicit instruction: "Do NOT create README, docs, or project plans. Write
   only executable code."

RESEARCH SUMMARY: AI AGENT ORCHESTRATION FRAMEWORKS - PREVENTING DRIFT & ENFORCING TASK FOCUS
